content: The value of ${ }^{47} \mathrm{C}_4+\sum\limits_{\mathrm{j}=1}^5{ }^{(52-\mathrm{j})} \mathrm{C}_3$ is
The number of ways in which 6 boys and 4 girls can be seated around a round table such that 2 special boys and a special girl never sit together is
explanation:
Number of ways in which 10 people can sit around a round table

$$ =(10-1)!=9!=362880 $$

Let us consider the 2 special boys and 1 special girl as one unit.

Now, this one unit is to be arranged with the remaining 7 people ( 4 boys and 3 girls), i.e. a total of 8 units to be arranged around a round table.

Ways to arrange 8 units around a round table $=(8-1)!=7!=5040$

The 2 special boys and 1 special girl can be arranged within the group in $3!=6$ ways

$\therefore \quad$ The total number of arrangements where the 3 special people sit together $=5040 \times 6=30240$ Hence, the required number of arrangements where the 2 special boys and 1 special girl do not sit together $=362880-30240=332640$

content: The number of ways in which a team of 11 players can be formed out of 25 players, if 6 out of them are always to be included and 5 of them are always to be excluded, is
explanation:
Total players $=25$

Since 5 players are always excluded.

$$ \therefore \quad \text { No. of players }=25-5=20 $$

Out of these, 6 players are always included.

$$ \therefore $$  Remaining players available for selection

$$ =20-6=14 $$

Since 6 players are already selected.

$$ \therefore $$  No of players to be selected $=11-6=5$

$$ \therefore $$  No. of ways $={ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_5$

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\frac{14 \times 13 \times 12 \times 11 \times 10}{1 \times 2 \times 3 \times 4 \times 5}=2002 $$

content: There are 11 points in a plane of which 5 points are collinear. Then the total number of distinct quadrilaterals with vertices at these points is
explanation:
Number of ways to choose 4 points from 11 points is ${ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_4=330$

A quadrilateral cannot be formed if all 4 chosen points are collinear.

Case I: 3 points from the 5 collinear points and 1 point from the remaining 6 points

$$ \begin{aligned} & ={ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3 \times{ }^6 \mathrm{C}_1 \\ & =10 \times 6=60 \end{aligned} $$

Case II: All 4 points from the 5 collinear points $={ }^5 \mathrm{C}_4=5$

$$ \therefore $$  Total number of distinct quadrilaterals

$$ =330-60-5=265 $$

content: If ${ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_4+{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_5+{ }^{16} \mathrm{C}_6+{ }^{17} \mathrm{C}_7+{ }^{18} \mathrm{C}_8={ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}$, then the value of $r$ is equal to
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & { }^{15} \mathrm{C}_4+{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_5+{ }^{16} \mathrm{C}_6+{ }^{17} \mathrm{C}_7+{ }^{18} \mathrm{C}_8={ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \Rightarrow{ }^{16} \mathrm{C}_5+{ }^{16} \mathrm{C}_6+{ }^{17} \mathrm{C}_7+{ }^{18} \mathrm{C}_8={ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \\ &\quad \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \,\,\,\,\,\ \ldots\left[\because{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}+{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}-1}={ }^{\mathrm{n}+1} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow{ }^{17} \mathrm{C}_6+{ }^{17} \mathrm{C}_7+{ }^{18} \mathrm{C}_8={ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \Rightarrow{ }^{18} \mathrm{C}_7+{ }^{18} \mathrm{C}_8={ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \Rightarrow{ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_8={ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \Rightarrow{ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}={ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_8 \text { or }{ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_{19-8} . \\ & \Rightarrow{ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}={ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_8 \text { or }{ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_{11} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=8 \text { or } 11 \end{aligned} $$

content: A family consisting of a mother, father and their 8 children ( 4 boys and 4 girls) are to be seated at a round table in a party. How many ways can this be done if the mother and father sit together and the males and females alternate?
explanation:
Since the arrangement is around a round table, and the mother and father must sit together, treat them as a single unit (MF block).

Additionally, the seating must alternate between males and females. Fix the MF block in a position where it fits into a valid alternating sequence.

One such fixed alternating pattern is:

$$ M F-G-B-G-B-G-B-G-B $$

Girls can occupy the seats in 4 ! ways, and boys can occupy the seats in 4 ! ways. Therefore, the total number of ways $=4!\times 4!=576$

content: If four digit numbers are formed by using the digits $1,2,3,4,5,6,7$ without repetition, then out of these numbers, the numbers exactly divisible by 25 are
explanation:
For divisibility by 25 , last two digits are 00,25 , 50, or 75.

Case I: Numbers ending in 25.

$1^{\text {st }}$ digit can be selected in 5 ways
$2^{\text {nd }}$ digit can be selected in 4 ways

$\therefore \quad$ Total number of such numbers $=5 \times 4=20$

Case II: Numbers ending in 75.

$1^{\text {st }}$ digit can be selected in 5 ways

$2^{\text {nd }}$ digit can be selected in 4 ways

$\therefore \quad$ Total numbers $=20+20=40$

content: 21 friends were invited for a party. Two round tables can accommodate 12 and 9 friends each, The number of ways of the seating arrangements of friends is …..
explanation:
Total number of ways selecting 12 friends out

$$ \text { of } 21={ }^{21} \mathrm{C}_{12}=\frac{21!}{12!\times 9!} $$

Then the remaining 9 friends will automatically be seated at the table accommodating 9 friends

$$ \Rightarrow{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_9=1 $$

Total number of arrangements for 12 friends at the round table

$$ \begin{aligned} & =(12-1)! \\ & =11! \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Similarly, for a } 9 \text { friends } & =(9-1)! \\ & =8! \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Total ways of arrangements

$$ \begin{aligned} & ={ }^{21} \mathrm{C}_{12} \times{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_9 \times 11!\times 8! \\ & =\frac{21!}{12!\times 9!} \times 11!\times 8!=\frac{35}{9} \times 19! \end{aligned} $$

content: The greatest possible number of points of intersection of 8 distinct straight lines and 4 distinct circles is
explanation:
Intersection among 8 straight lines:

Any 2 lines can intersect at most once.

$\therefore \quad$ Maximum number of points of intersection

$$ \begin{aligned} & ={ }^8 \mathrm{C}_2 \\ & =\frac{8!}{2!\times 6!} \\ & =28 \end{aligned} $$

Intersection among 4 circles:

Any 2 circles can intersect at most in 2 points.

$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Number of circle pairs } & ={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2 \\ & =\frac{4!}{2!\times 2!}=6 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Maximum number of points of intersection

$$ =2 \times 6=12 $$

Intersection between 8 lines and 4 circles:

Each line can intersect a circle at most in 2 points.

$\therefore \quad$ Number of line-circle pairs $=8 \times 4=32$

$\therefore \quad$ Maximum intersection points $=32 \times 2=64$

$\therefore \quad$ Total maximum number of points of intersection

$$ =28+12+64=104 $$

content: 4 red balls and 5 green balls are selected from $n$ balls. If the sum of both the selections is greater than ${ }^{n+1} C_4$ then the value of $n$ is equal to
explanation:
4 red balls can be selected from $n$ balls in ${ }^n C_4$ ways

5 green balls can be selected from n balls in ${ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_5$ ways

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Sum of both the selections is greater than }{ }^{n+1} C_4\\ &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow{ }^n C_4+{ }^n C_5>{ }^{n+1} C_4 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{n!}{4!(n-4)!}+\frac{n!}{5!(n-5)!}>\frac{(n+1)!}{4!(n+1-4)!} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{n!}{4!(n-4)(n-5)!}+\frac{n!}{5 \times 4!(n-5)!}>\frac{(n+1) n!}{4!(n-3)!} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{n!}{4!(n-5)!}\left[\frac{1}{n-4}+\frac{1}{5}\right]>\frac{(n+1) n!}{4!(n-3)(n-4)(n-5)!} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{5+(n-4)}{5(n-4)}>\frac{n+1}{(n-3)(n-4)} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{n+1}{5}>\frac{n+1}{n-3} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{5}>\frac{1}{n-3} \\ & \Rightarrow n-3>5 \\ & \Rightarrow n>8 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: A regular polygon has 20 sides. The number of triangles that can be drawn by using the vertices but not using the sides are
explanation:
Total triangles $={ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_3$

Triangles with two sides of the polygon $=20$ (formed by 3 consecutive vertices)

Triangles with one side of the polygon $=20 \times 16$

$\therefore \quad$ Required number of triangles

$$ \begin{aligned} & ={ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_3-20-20 \times 16 \\ & =\frac{20 \times 19 \times 18}{1 \times 2 \times 3}-20-16 \times 20 \\ & =20(57-1-16) \\ & =20 \times 40 \\ & =800 \end{aligned} $$

content: The domain of the function $\mathrm{f}(x)={ }^{7-x} \mathrm{P}_{x-1}$ is
explanation:
$$ f(x)={ }^{7-x} P_{x-1} $$

$\mathrm{f}(x)$ is defined, if

$$ \begin{aligned} & x-1 \leq 7-x \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x \leq 8 \\ & \Rightarrow x \leq 4 \end{aligned} $$

${ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{r}}$ is defined if $\mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{r} \in \mathrm{W}$ and $\mathrm{r} \leq \mathrm{n}$.

$$ \therefore \quad x=\{1,2,3,4\} $$

content: Total number of 3-digit numbers, whose g.c.d with 36 is 2 , is
explanation:
$\operatorname{gcd}(n, 36)=2\,\,\,\,\,\, …[Given]$

Where, n is 3 -digit number.

Let, $\mathrm{A}=3$-digit numbers divisible by 2 , but not 3 or 4.

Total 3-digit numbers divisible by 2

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{998-100}{2}+1 \\ & =\frac{898}{2}+1 \\ & =449+1=450 \end{aligned} $$

Total numbers divisible by 6

$$ =\frac{996-102}{6}+1 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{894}{6}+1 \\ & =149+1=150 \end{aligned} $$

Total numbers divisible by 4

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{996-100}{4}+1 \\ & =\frac{896}{4}+1 \\ & =224+1 \\ & =225 \end{aligned} $$

Total numbers divisible by 12

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{996-108}{12}+1 \\ & =74+1=75 \end{aligned} $$

By inclusion - exclusion principle,

$$ \begin{aligned} & |A|=450-(150+225-75) \\ & =450-300=150 \end{aligned} $$

content: The number of ways, in which 6 boys and 5 girls can sit at a round table, if no two girls are to sit together, is
explanation:
6 boys can seat at a round table in $(6-1)!=5!$ ways. Now, no two girls sit together, i.e., 5 girls are to be arranged in 6 empty seats between two consecutive boys and number of such arrangements will be ${ }^6 \mathrm{P}_5=6$ !

Hence, required number of ways

$$\begin{aligned} & =5!\times 6! \\ & =120 \times 720 \\ & =86400 \end{aligned}$$

content: A five digit number divisible by 3 is to be formed using the digits $0,1,2,3,4,5$ without repetition, then the total number of ways this can be done is
explanation:
We know that a five digit number is divisible by 3, if and only if sum of its digits $(=15)$ is divisible by 3. Therefore, we should not use 0 and 3 in a same number while forming the five digit numbers.

Now,

i. In case we do not use 0 , the five digit number can be formed (using digits $1,2,3,4,5$ ) in ${ }^5 \mathrm{P}_5=120$ ways.

ii. In case we do not use 3 , the five digit number can be formed (using digits $0,1,2,4,5$ ) in ${ }^5 \mathrm{P}_5-{ }^4 \mathrm{P}_4=5!-4!=120-24=96$ ways

$\therefore \quad$ The total number of such 5 digit number $=120+96=216$

content: Eight chairs are numbered 1 to 8 . Two women and three men wish to occupy one chair each. First the women choose chairs from amongst the chairs marked 1 to 4 , and then the men select the chairs from amongst the remaining. The number of possible arrangements is
explanation:
Two women can be made to sit on chairs marked 1 to 4 in ${ }^4 \mathrm{P}_2$ ways and then three men can be seated in 6 available seats in ${ }^6 \mathrm{P}_3$ ways.

$\therefore \quad$ number of possible arrangements is ${ }^4 \mathrm{P}_2 \times{ }^6 \mathrm{P}_3$.

content: There are 3 sections in a question paper and each section contains 5 questions. A candidate has to answer a total of 5 questions, choosing at least one question from each section. Then the number of ways, in which the candidate can choose the questions, is
explanation:
Total number of ways

$$\begin{aligned} &={ }^5 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2 \\ &+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3 \times \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2 \times \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2 \\ &+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2 \times \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3 \times \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_1 \\ &=5 \times 5 \times 10+5 \times 10 \times 10+5 \times 10 \times 5 \\ &+10 \times 5 \times 10+10 \times 10 \times 5+10 \times 5 \times 5 \\ &=250+500+250+500+500+250 \\ &= 2250 \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of arrangements, of the letters of the word MANAMA in which two M's do not appear adjacent, is
explanation:
There are 6 letters.

M repeats 2 times,

A repeats 3 times.

We first arrange all letters except 2 M's in $\frac{4!}{3!}$ $=4$ ways

These 4 letters create 5 gaps, where we can arrange $2 \mathrm{M}^{\prime}$ s in $\frac{{ }^5 \mathrm{p}_2}{2!}=10$ ways

$\therefore \quad$ Required number of arrangements $=4 \times 10=40$

content: _________ numbers greater than a million can be formed with the digits 2, 3, 0, 3, 4, 2, 3.
explanation:
All seven-digit numbers formed with the given digits are greater than a million.

Note that the digit at millions place cannot be 0. It can be any one of the digits $2,3,4$.

$\therefore \quad$ It can be any one of the digits $2,3,4$.

Case I :

Digit at millions place is ' 2 '.

Remaining 6 digits can be arranged in $\frac{6!}{3!}$ $=120$ ways.

Case II :

Digit at millions place is ' 3 '.

Remaining 6 digits can be arranged in $\frac{6!}{2!\times 2!}$ $=180$ ways.

Case III :

Digit at millions place is ' 4 '.

Remaining 6 digits can be arraigned in $\frac{6!}{31 \times 2!}$ $=60$ ways

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore\text { Required number of numbers } & =120+180+60 \\ & =360 \end{aligned}$$

content: Words of length 10 are formed by using the letters A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J. Let $x$ be number of such words where no letter is repeated and $y$ be number of such words where exactly two letters are repeated twice and no other letter is repeated, then the value of $\frac{y}{x}$ is
explanation:
Letters are A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J

Number of words that can be formed by 10 letters $=10!\times{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{10}$ $x=10$ !

Now, for repetition of two letters.

Two letters can be selected in ${ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_2$ ways which are used twice in the word and remaining 6 letters can be selected from 8 letters in ${ }^8 \mathrm{C}_6$ ways.

Hence, Number of words can be formed

$$\begin{aligned} & ={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_6 \times \frac{10!}{2!\times 2!} \\ \therefore \quad y & ={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_6 \times \frac{10!}{2!\times 2!} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{y}{x} & =\frac{{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_2 \times \frac{10!}{2!\times 2!}}{10!} \\ & =\frac{{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_6}{2!\times 2!} \\ & =\frac{45 \times 28}{4} \\ & =315 \end{aligned}$$

content: Consider a group of 5 boys and 7 girls. The number of different teams, consisting of 2 boys and 3 girls that can be formed from this group if there are two specific girls A and B , who refuse to be the members of the same team, is
explanation:
There are 5 boys and 7 girls in a class. Total number of ways $={ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^7 \mathrm{C}_3=350$

If both girls $A$ and $B$ are in the same team, then ${ }^5 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2=50$

$\therefore \quad$ Required number of ways

$=$ Total number of ways $-$ Both girls A and B are in the same team

$$=350-50=300$$

content: Five persons $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}$ and E are seated in a circular arangement, if each of them is given a hat of one of the three colours red, blue and green, then the number of ways, of distributing the hats such that the person seated in adjacent seats get different coloured hats, is
explanation:
Given, 5 persons having 5 hats of colour red, blue and green

$\therefore \quad$ i.e., 3 colours Maximum 2 hats of same colour can be used.

$\therefore \quad$ Number of ways of selecting single colour hat out of 3 colours $={ }^3 C_1$ ways.

$\therefore \quad$ Single colour hat is distributed in 5 persons in ${ }^5 C_1$ ways Also, number of ways to distribute alternative coloured hat to adjacent person $={ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Required number of ways } & ={ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1 \\ & =3 \times 5 \times 2 \\ & =30 \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of ways in which 5 boys and 3 girls can be seated on a round table, if a particular boy $B_1$ and a particular girl $G_1$ never sit adjacent to each other, is
explanation:
First, we arrange 4 boys and 2 girls (excluding $B_1$ and $G_1$ ) around the table, which can be done in 5 ! ways.

In any such arrangement, $B_1$ and $G_1$ can be arranged in 6 available gaps in ${ }^6 \mathrm{P}_2=6 \times 5$ ways.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Total number of arrangements } & =5!\times 6 \times 5 \\ & =6!\times 5 \end{aligned}$$

content: A committee of 11 members is to be formed from 8 males and 5 females. If $m$ is the number of ways the committee is formed with at least 6 males and $n$ is the number of ways the committee is formed with at least 3 females, then
explanation:
A committee of 11 members is to be formed form 8 males and 5 females.

$\therefore \quad$ When atleast 6 males are included, the committee contains

(6 Males and 5 females), ( 7 males and 4 females), (8 males and 3 Females)

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \text { Required number of ways } \\ & ={ }^8 \mathrm{C}_6 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_5+{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_7 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_4+{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_8 \times{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~m}=78 \\ & \text { When atleast } 3 \text { females are included, the } \\ & \text { committee contains } \\ & \text { (3 females and } 8 \text { males), (4 females and } 7 \text { males), } \\ & \text { (5 females and } 6 \text { males) } \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Required number of ways } \\ & ={ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3 \times{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_8+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_4 \times{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_7+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_5 \times{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_6 \\ & =78 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{n}=78\\ \therefore\quad \mathrm{m=n=78} \end{array}$$

content: The number of four letter words that can be formed using letters of the word BARRACK
explanation:
Word BARRACK has 7 letters in which ' $A$ ' and ' $R$ ' repeats twice.

Case I :

All four letters are different. (B, A, R, C, K)

$\therefore \quad$ No. of letters $={ }^5 \mathrm{C}_4 \times 4!=120$

Case II :

' $R$ ' repeats twice and remaining letter three letters are different (B, A, C, K)

$\therefore \quad$ No. of letters $={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2 \times \frac{4!}{2!}=72$

Case III :

' A ' repeats twice and remaining letter three letters are different (B, R, C, K)

$\therefore \quad$ No. of letters $={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2 \times \frac{4!}{2!}=72$

Case IV :

Both ' $A$ ' and ' $R$ ' repeat twice.

$\therefore \quad$ No. of letters $=\frac{4!}{2 \times 2!}=6$

$\therefore \quad$ Total no. of letters form $=120+72+72+6$ $=270$

content: Number of different nine digit numbers, that can be formed from the digits in the number 223355888 by rearranging its digits, so that the odd digits occupy even positions, is
explanation:
Since $3,3,5,5$, i.e., odd digits occupy even positions and $2,2,8,8,8$ occupy remaining 5 odd places.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Required number of ways } & =\frac{4!}{2!2!} \cdot \frac{5!}{2!3!} \\ & =6 \times 10=60 \end{aligned}$$

content: If 3 books on Physics, 2 books on Chemistry and 4 books on Mathematics are to be arranged on a shelf so that all the Physics books are together and all the Mathematics books are together, then the number of such arrangements is
explanation:
There are 3 books on physics, 2 books on Chemistry and 4 books on Mathematics. All Physics books and Mathematics books are kept together.

$\therefore \quad$ All Physics books will be considered as 1 unit and all Mathematics books are also considered as 1 unit.

$\therefore \quad$ Physics, Mathematics and 2 Chemistry books can be arranged in 4 ! ways.

Also, 3 Physics and 4 Mathematics books can be arranged themselves in $3!\times 4$ ! ways

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Total number of arrangements } & =4!\times 3!\times 4! \\ & =3456 \end{aligned}$$

content: If in a regular polygon, the number of diagonals are 54, then the number of sides of the polygon are
explanation:
Number of diagonals in a polygon of n sides is $${ }^{\mathrm{n}} C_2-\mathrm{n}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & { }^{\mathrm{n}} C_2-\mathrm{n}=54 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)}{2}-\mathrm{n}=54 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^2-3 \mathrm{n}-108=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(n-12)(\mathrm{n}+9)=0 \end{aligned}$$

But, number of sides cannot be negative,

$$\therefore$$ n = 12

content: A linguistic club consists of 6 girls and 4 boys. A team of 4 members is to be selected from this group including the selection of a leader (from among these 4 members) for the team. If the team has to include at most one boy, the number of ways of selecting the team is
explanation:
Case I : No boy is included.

Selecting 4 girls from 6 girls $$={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4$$

Selecting 1 captain from selected members $$={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1$$

Total number of ways $$={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1=60$$

Case II: One boy is included.

Selecting 3 girls and 1 boy from given members

$$={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_3 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1 \text {. }$$

Selecting 1 captain from the selected members $$={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1 \text {. }$$

Total Number of ways $$={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_3 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1=320$$.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Total Number of ways $$=320+60=380$$.

content: Five students are selected from $$n$$ students such that the ratio of number of ways in which 2 particular students are selected to the number of ways 2 particular students not selected is $$2: 3$$. Then, the value of $$n$$ is
explanation:
Here, 5 students are to be selected from $$n$$ students.

When 2 specified students are included, then remaining 3 students can be selected $$(n-2)$$ students

$$\therefore$$ Number of ways of selecting 3 student from $$n$$ students $$={ }^{(n-2)} C_3$$

Now, when 2 specified students are not included, then 5 students can be selected from the remaining $$(n-2)$$ students.

$$\therefore$$ Number of ways of selecting 5 students from $$(n-2)$$ students $$={ }^{n-2} C_5$$

According to question,

$$\begin{array}{ll} & { }^{n-2} C_3:{ }^{n-2} C_5=2: 3 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{(n-2) !}{3 !(n-5) !} \times \frac{5 !(n-7) !}{(n-2) !}=2: 3 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{5 !}{3 !} \frac{(n-7) !}{(n-5) !}=\frac{2}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{5 \times 4}{(n-5)(n-6)}=\frac{2}{3} \end{array}$$

$$\therefore$$ Then, value of $$n=11$$

content: Five persons $$\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}$$ and $$\mathrm{E}$$ are seated in a circular arrangement. If each of them is given a cap of one of the three colours red, blue and green, then the number of ways of distributing the caps such that the persons seated in adjacent seats get different coloured caps, is
explanation:
There are 5 caps and 3 colours.

$$\therefore \quad$$ At least one colour will get repeated.

As adjacent caps should be of different colours, no colour can repeat thrice.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Exactly two colours will repeat twice.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Colour of the caps are selected in 3 ways as follows:

Red-Red-Green-Green-Blue,

Red-Red-Green-Blue-Blue,

Red-Green-Green-Blue-Blue.

Now, while distributing the caps from above combinations, we choose any one of the 5 persons and give single colour cap. And remaining four caps can be distributed in alternate colour sequence, clock-wise or anticlock-wise.

This can be done in $$5 \times 2$$ ways.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Required number of ways $$=3 \times 5 \times 2=30$$.

content: The number of words that can be formed by using the letters of the word CALCULATE such that each word starts and ends with a consonant, are
explanation:
Word CALCULATE has 9 letters. Out of which 'C' repeats 2 times, 'A' repeats 2 times, 'L' repeats 2 times, 'E', 'U' and 'T' repeats once.

$$\therefore \quad$$ There are 5 consonants and 4 vowels. Two consonants out of 5 can take start and end position of the word in $${ }^5 \mathrm{P}_2$$ ways.

And remaining 7 letters can take remaining 7 positions in 7 ! ways. Also, 'C', 'A' and 'L' repeats twice each.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The required number of words that can be formed $$=\frac{5 p_2 \times 7 !}{2 ! \times 2 ! \times 2 !}=\frac{5 \times 4 \times 3 ! \times 7 !}{3 ! \times 2 \times 2 \times 2}=\frac{5 \times 7 !}{2}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}$$ denotes the number of triangles which can be formed using the vertices of regular polygon of $$\mathrm{n}$$ sides and $$T_{n+1}-T_n=21$$, then $$\mathrm{n}=$$
explanation:
According to the given condition, $$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_3$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}+1}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=21 \Rightarrow{ }^{\mathrm{n}+1} \mathrm{C}_3-{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_3=21$$

Note that $$\mathrm{n}=7$$ satisfies the above condition.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Option (B) is correct.

content: The teacher wants to arrange 5 students on the platform such that the boy $$B_1$$ occupies second position and the girls $$G_1$$ and $$G_2$$ are always adjacent to each other, then the number of such arrangements is
explanation:
There are 5 positions.

Given that $$B_1$$ occupies $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ position

$$\therefore B_1$$ can be arranged in 1 way.

As $$\mathrm{G}_1$$ and $$\mathrm{G}_2$$ are always together, none of them can take $$1^{\text {st }}$$ position.

$$\therefore \mathrm{G}_1, \mathrm{G}_2$$ and one of the remaining students can be arranged on $$3^{\text {rd }}, 4^{\text {th }}$$ and $$5^{\text {th }}$$ position when $$\mathrm{G}_1$$ and $$\mathrm{G}_2$$ are always together in $$2 ! \times 2$$ ! Ways.

And remaining 2 students can be arranged in 2 ! Ways.

$$\therefore$$ The required number of arrangements

$$\begin{aligned} & =2 ! \times 2 ! \times 2 ! \\ & =8 \end{aligned}$$

content: Five students are to be arranged on a platform such that the boy $$B_1$$ occupies the second position and such that the girl $$G_1$$ is always adjacent to the girl $$G_2$$. Then, the number of such possible arrangements is
explanation:
There are 5 positions.

Given that $$\mathrm{B}_1$$ occupies $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ position

$$\therefore \quad B_1$$ can be arranged in 1 way. As $$\mathrm{G}_1$$ and $$\mathrm{G}_2$$ are always together, none of them can take $$1^{\text {st }}$$ position.

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{G}_1, \mathrm{G}_2$$ and one of the remaining students can be arranged on $$3^{\text {rd }}, 4^{\text {th }}$$ and $$5^{\text {th }}$$ position when $$G_1$$ and $$\mathrm{G}_2$$ are always together in $$2 ! \times 2$$ ! Ways. And remaining 2 students can be arranged in 2 ! Ways.

$$\therefore \quad$$ The required number of arrangements $$=2 ! \times 2 ! \times 2 !=8$$

content: A group consists of 8 boys and 5 girls, then the number of committees of 5 persons that can be formed, if committee consists of at least 2 girls and at most 2 boys, are
explanation:
A committee of 5 is to be formed from 8 boys and 5 girls so that it consists of at least 2 girls and at most 2 boys.

Total number of ways

$$\begin{aligned} & =(\text { Five girls })+(4 \text { girls })(1 \text { boy })+(3 \text { girls })(2 \text { boys }) \\ & ={ }^5 C_5+{ }^5 C_4 \times{ }^8 C_1+{ }^5 C_3 \times{ }^8 C_2 \\ & =1+(5 \times 8)+(10 \times 28) \\ & =1+40+280 \\ & =321 \end{aligned}$$

content: A linguistic club of a certain Institute consists of 6 girls and 4 boys. A team of 4 members to be selected from this group including the selection of a Captain (from among these 4 members) for the team. If the team has to include atmost one boy, the number of ways of selecting the team is
explanation:
Case I: No boy is included.

Selecting 4 girls from 6 girls $$={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4$$

Selecting 1 captain from selected members $$={ }^4 C_1$$

Total number of ways $$={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1=60$$

Case II: One boy is included.

Selecting 3 girls and 1 boy from given members $$={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_3 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1$$.

Selecting 1 captain from the selected members $$={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1$$.

Total Number of ways $$={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_3 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1=320$$.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Total Number of ways $$=320+60=380$$.

content: If at the end of certain meeting, everyone had shaken hands with everyone else, it was found that 45 handshakes were exchanged, then the number of members present at the meeting, are
explanation:
Let 'n' be the number of members in the meeting

$$\therefore \quad$$ Total number of handshakes $$={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_2$$

$$\therefore \quad{ }^n C_2=45$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{n !}{2 !(n-2) !}=45 \\ & \frac{n(n-1)(n-2) !}{2 \times(n-2) !}=45 \\ & n(n-1)=90 \\ \therefore \quad & n^2-n-90=0 \\ & n=10 \text { or } n=-9 \text { (not possible) } \\ \therefore \quad & n=10 \end{array}$$

content: If a question paper consists of 11 questions divided into two sections I and II. Section I consists of 6 questions and section II consists of 5 questions, then the number of different ways can student select 6 questions, taking at least 2 questions from each section, is
explanation:
To solve this problem, we will consider the different ways in which the student can select questions from two distinct sections under the given constraint that at least 2 questions must be selected from each section. We can break it down into different cases and compute the number of possibilities for each case.

There are three cases to consider:

  • Case 1: Selecting 2 questions from Section I and 4 questions from Section II.
  • Case 2: Selecting 3 questions from Section I and 3 questions from Section II.
  • Case 3: Selecting 4 questions from Section I and 2 questions from Section II.

Then we will add the number of ways from all the cases, as these are mutually exclusive events.

Let's calculate for each case:

Case 1: Choose 2 questions from Section I and 4 questions from Section II.

The number of ways to choose 2 questions from 6 in Section I is given by $C(6, 2)$, and the number of ways to choose 4 questions from 5 in Section II is given by $C(5, 4)$.

Therefore, the total number of ways for Case 1 = $C(6, 2) \times C(5, 4)$.

Case 2: Choose 3 questions from Section I and 3 questions from Section II.

The number of ways to choose 3 questions from 6 in Section I is given by $C(6, 3)$, and the number of ways to choose 3 questions from 5 in Section II is given by $C(5, 3)$.

Therefore, the total number of ways for Case 2 = $C(6, 3) \times C(5, 3)$.

Case 3: Choose 4 questions from Section I and 2 questions from Section II.

The number of ways to choose 4 questions from 6 in Section I is given by $C(6, 4)$, and the number of ways to choose 2 questions from 5 in Section II is given by $C(5, 2)$.

Therefore, the total number of ways for Case 3 = $C(6, 4) \times C(5, 2)$.

Let's calculate the number of combinations for each case:

  • For Case 1: $C(6, 2) \times C(5, 4) = \frac{6!}{2!(6-2)!} \times \frac{5!}{4!(5-4)!} = 15 \times 5 = 75$
  • For Case 2: $C(6, 3) \times C(5, 3) = \frac{6!}{3!(6-3)!} \times \frac{5!}{3!(5-3)!} = 20 \times 10 = 200$
  • For Case 3: $C(6, 4) \times C(5, 2) = \frac{6!}{4!(6-4)!} \times \frac{5!}{2!(5-2)!} = 15 \times 10 = 150$

Now to find the total number of different ways, we add the number from all three cases together:

Total number of ways = $75 + 200 + 150 = 425$

Thus, the total number of different ways a student can select 6 questions, taking at least 2 questions from each section, is 425, which corresponds to Option A.

content: A committee of 5 is to be formed out of 6 men and 4 ladies. The number of ways this can be done, when at most 2 ladies are included, is
explanation:
We need to find the number of ways to form a committee of 5 with at most 2 ladies. This means we have three possible scenarios:

  1. Scenario 1: No ladies

    2. We need to choose 5 men out of 6. This can be done in $$^6C_5 = \frac{6!}{5!1!} = 6$$ ways.

  2. Scenario 2: One lady

    3. We need to choose 1 lady out of 4 and 4 men out of 6. This can be done in $$^4C_1 \times ^6C_4 = \frac{4!}{1!3!} \times \frac{6!}{4!2!} = 4 \times 15 = 60$$ ways.

  3. Scenario 3: Two ladies

    4. We need to choose 2 ladies out of 4 and 3 men out of 6. This can be done in $$^4C_2 \times ^6C_3 = \frac{4!}{2!2!} \times \frac{6!}{3!3!} = 6 \times 20 = 120$$ ways.

Therefore, the total number of ways to form the committee is 6 + 60 + 120 = 186.

So the correct answer is Option B: 186

content: Out of 7 consonants and 4 vowels, the number of words consisting of 3 consonants and 2 vowels are
explanation:
We have to choose 3 consonants and 2 vowels from 7 consonants and 4 vowels.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Number of words } & =\left({ }^7 C_3\right) \times\left({ }^4 C_2\right) \times 5! \\ & =\frac{7!}{3!4!} \times \frac{4!}{2!2!} \times 5!=25200 \end{aligned}$$

content: The numbers can be formed using the digits $$1,2,3,4,3,2,1$$ so that odd digits always occupy odd places in __________ ways.
explanation:
First, we should understand that the given digits are $$1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 1$$, and the requirement is that odd digits must occupy odd places. This means positions 1, 3, 5, etc., should be filled with odd digits. Here, the odd digits available are $$1, 3, 1, 3$$ and the even digits are $$2, 4, 2$$.

The total places we have can be observed as 7 (since seven digits are mentioned), indicating there are four odd positions (1st, 3rd, 5th, 7th) and three even positions (2nd, 4th, 6th).

To solve the problem, we can use permutations to assign digits to the appropriate positions while following the given condition.

Step 1: Assign Odd Digits to Odd Positions

We have 4 odd digits $$1, 3, 1, 3$$ and 4 odd positions to fill. Here, notice we have repetitions of 1 and 3. Therefore, the number of ways to arrange these odd digits in odd positions considering the repetitions can be calculated as:

$$\frac{4!}{2! \cdot 2!}$$

This accounts for 4 factorial ways to arrange 4 items divided by the repetition of 2 factorial for both 1 and 3 to eliminate overcounting due to identical items.

$$\frac{4!}{2! \cdot 2!} = \frac{24}{4} = 6$$ ways

Step 2: Assign Even Digits to Even Positions

There are 3 even digits $$2, 4, 2$$ and 3 even positions to fill. Since the digits 2 are repeated, the permutations will be reduced by considering this repetition:

$$\frac{3!}{2!}$$

This equation calculates the number of ways to arrange 3 items where 2 items are identical, hence the division by 2 factorial.

$$\frac{3!}{2!} = \frac{6}{2} = 3$$ ways

Overall Calculation

The overall number of ways to form the numbers with the given digits condition is obtained by multiplying the ways to place odd and even digits:

Odd digits arrangement × Even digits arrangement = $$6 \times 3 = 18$$

Therefore, the numbers can be formed in such a way that odd digits always occupy odd places in 18 ways.

So, the correct answer is Option B: 18.

content: A polygon has 44 diagonals. Then the number of sides of the polygon are
explanation:
Number of diagonals of '$$n$$' sided polygons $$={ }^n C_2-n$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore{ }^n C_2-n=44 \\ & \frac{n !}{2 !(n-2) !}-n=44 \Rightarrow n(n-1)-2 n=88 \\ & \therefore n^2-3 n-88=0 \Rightarrow(n-11)(n+8)=0 \Rightarrow n=11 \end{aligned}$$ $$\ldots[n \in N]$$

content: For a set of five true or false questions, no student has written the all correct answers and no two students have given the same sequence of answers. The maximum number of students in the class for this to be possible is
explanation:
Each of the five questions can be solved in two ways.

$$\therefore$$ Maximum number of wrong answers.

$$=(2\times2\times2\times2\times2)-1=31$$

content: The number of ways in which 8 different pearls can be arranged to form a necklace is
explanation: Number of necklaces formed from 8 different pearls $$=\frac{(8-1) !}{2}=7 \times 6 \times 5 \times 4 \times 3=2520$$

content: If $$\frac{n !}{2 !(n-2) !}$$ and $$\frac{n !}{4 !(n-4) !}$$ are in the ratio $$2: 1$$, then $$n=$$
explanation:
We have $$\frac{n !}{2 !(n-2) !} \times \frac{4 !(n-4) !}{n !}=\frac{2}{1}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \frac{(4 \times 3)}{(n-2)(n-3)}=2 & \Rightarrow n^2-5 n+6=6 \\ \therefore \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-5)=0 & \Rightarrow n=5\end{array}$$

content: All the letters of the word 'ABRACADABRA' are arranged in different possible ways. Then the number of such arrangements in which the vowels are together is
explanation:
Given the word "ABRACADABRA":

  1. Vowels : A, A, A, A, A (5 A's)
  2. Consonants : B, R, C, D, B, R (2 B's, 2 R's, 1 C, and 1 D)

When considering arrangements where the vowels are together, we can treat all the vowels as one single entity.

Thus, the entities for our purpose are :

Entity of Vowels (AAAAA), B, R, C, D, B, R

Given the repetitions, the number of ways to arrange these 7 entities is : $ \text{Arrangements} = \frac{7!}{2! \times 2!} $

Since all the vowels are identical, there's only 1 way to arrange them within their entity.

Therefore, the total number of arrangements where vowels are together is : $\text{Total} = \frac{7!}{2! \times 2!} \times 1 = 1260 $

Thus, the correct answer is $1260$.
